text: Only 27.3% of participants completed a modified 9-week Couch-to-5k beginner running programme.
Previous musculoskeletal injury significantly increases the risk of new injury during a beginner running programme (OR 7.56, 95% CI 2.06–27.75).
19% of beginner runners reported musculoskeletal injuries during a 9-week Couch-to-5k programme.

Self-reported health scores improved significantly from baseline (73.1/100) to midpoint (81.2/100) of a beginner running programme, but gains did not persist significantly to programme end (79.7/100).
Half of beginner running programme participants self-reported pain or discomfort and 37.2% reported anxiety/depression at programme start, indicating high baseline health burdens in this population.
Couch-to-5k programmes predominantly attract females (81.8%), higher socio-economic status individuals, and people with existing running experience (64%), rather than diverse inactive populations.

Programme non-completion in beginner runners is linked to musculoskeletal injury, negative emotions, and rigid progressive programme design, suggesting programmes should include injury prevention advice and more flexible progression.
